title: Wet-felted shibori resist scarf
subtitle: Textured surface patterns from tied and pleated resist felting
excerpt: A merino scarf with raised surface texture created by tying pebbles, corks, or small wooden blocks into the roving before wet felting. Resisted areas felt less densely and produce distinctive pattern zones. No two pieces come out alike.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from Japanese shibori resist-dyeing principles adapted for wet felting, widely taught in UK and European felting workshops from the 2000s onward. Pebble-tie and pleat-resist methods are the most accessible adaptations. Primary references: contemporary felting workshop notes; no single public domain text.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre drawn into a long, loosely twisted ribbon, ready for spinning or felting.
Fine-stapled wool from Merino sheep. Felts readily and produces a smooth, dense surface.
The second stage of wet felting, where the pre-felted piece is agitated more vigorously to shrink and strengthen the fibres further.
An object or material placed within or around the wool before felting. It blocks or reduces fibre migration in the resisted area, creating pattern or texture.
Felting using warm water, soap, and agitation to cause wool fibres to interlock and shrink into a cohesive fabric.

content.text: Shibori is a Japanese resist technique from dyeing, adapted here for
wet felting
. Tie, pleat, or gather wool around a
resist
object and those areas felt less than the rest. The surface puckers and raises at each tied point. Every piece comes out different.
Wool: 100-120 g fine
merino
roving

in one or two colours. Resist objects: small pebbles, wine corks, or wooden buttons. You will also need rubber bands or string, felting soap, and hot water (60-65°C). The scarf finishes at about 150 cm x 20 cm after
fulling
. Lay out your wool about 30% larger to allow for shrinkage.
Method

Lay out the roving. On your bubble wrap (textured side up), lay the merino roving in two perpendicular layers, each approximately 215 cm x 30 cm. The first layer runs lengthwise, the second crosswise. Each layer should be even in thickness with no gaps. Wet thoroughly with hot soapy water, pressing the roving flat with your palms so all fibres are saturated. Cover with a second piece of bubble wrap or a cotton tea towel.

Pre-felt. Press and rub gently over the covered surface for 5-8 minutes until the roving begins to hold together and can be peeled from the bubble wrap without tearing. This is the pre-felt stage. The piece is not finished felt yet; it is firm enough to handle but still has give.
Tie the resists. While the pre-felt is still damp, place your
resist

objects on the surface of the scarf. For each pebble or cork, gather the pre-felt up and over it into a tight pouch and secure the neck with a rubber band or several tight wraps of string. Space the resists irregularly for the most organic result. You can tie 6-15 objects depending on their size and your design intention.

Full the scarf. Return the tied piece to the hot soapy water and continue agitating: roll in the bamboo mat, throw the rolled mat onto the work surface 20-30 times, unroll, turn 90 degrees, re-roll and repeat. Alternate hot and cold water rinses to encourage
fulling
. Continue for 20-30 minutes total, checking the density of the fabric between the resists: it should feel firm and not stretch easily when pulled.

Remove the resists. Snip the rubber bands or untie the string and remove each object. The fabric at the gather points will be less dense and puckered. This is the texture you have been building. Do not attempt to flatten these areas; they are the point of the technique.

Final rinse and dry. Rinse the scarf thoroughly in warm water until no soap remains. Squeeze gently; do not wring. Roll in a dry cotton tea towel to remove excess water. Lay flat to dry, gently stretching the edges to straighten the overall shape while leaving the resist texture undisturbed.

items.symptom: Resist areas are barely visible after finishing
items.cause: The resists were removed too early, before the fabric was fully fulled
items.fix: On the next piece, check that the fabric between resists is firm and non-stretchy before removing them. At minimum 25 minutes of fulling time.

items.symptom: Fabric tore when removing the resist
items.cause: The rubber band was tied directly in the pre-felt stage before enough felt strength had developed
items.fix: Pre-felt for a longer period before tying. The fabric should peel cleanly from the bubble wrap before you begin to tie.

items.symptom: Scarf is thicker in some areas than others
items.cause: The initial roving layout was uneven
items.fix: Lay each roving layer with care, pulling wisps to fill thin spots before wetting. Hold the dry layout up to a window; thin patches will show as translucent areas.

items.symptom: Scarf is too stiff after drying
items.cause: Over-fulled, or soap not fully rinsed out
items.fix: Soak in warm water with a few drops of hair conditioner for 10 minutes, then rinse thoroughly and dry flat. This relaxes over-hardened merino.

content.text: Care and wearing

Hand wash in cool water with a small amount of wool wash. Do not wring or agitate: the scarf is already felted and will not shrink further under gentle handling, but rough treatment will cause further texture development. Roll in a towel and dry flat. The raised puckered areas at the old
resist
points will relax slightly over the first few wears and then stabilise.
